claim_text: Transcription factor EB induces transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines in macrophages infected with Staphylococcus aureus.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: SUPPORT

The abstract with doc_id 24177706 provides direct evidence supporting the claim: it states that TFEB (the mammalian homolog of HLH-30 mentioned in the worm study) is rapidly activated in murine macrophages upon S. aureus infection and is required for the proper transcriptional induction of several pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. This directly aligns with the claim that TFEB induces transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines in macrophages infected with Staphylococcus aureus.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract by [doc_id 25677651] directly supports the claim by demonstrating that activation of liver X receptors (LXRs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) prevents bacterial-induced macrophage apoptosis, and that LXR and RXR agonists induce the expression of antiapoptotic regulators and inhibit expression of proapoptotic regulators in macrophages. Additionally, the abstract by [doc_id 4388470] provides evidence that avian somatic cells possess an inherent sex identity, which is relevant to the claim as it suggests that transcription factors can have cell-autonomous effects on cell fate decisions.

VERDICT: SUPPORT

The abstract explicitly states that "TFEB was also rapidly activated in murine macrophages upon S. aureus infection and was required for proper transcriptional induction of several proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines." This directly supports the claim that TFEB induces transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines in macrophages infected with Staphylococcus aureus.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract directly supports the claim by stating that "TFEB was required for proper transcriptional induction of several proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines" upon Staphylococcus aureus infection in murine macrophages. This indicates that TFEB, also known as Transcription Factor EB, induces the transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines in macrophages infected with S. aureus.

VERDICT: SUPPORT

The abstract with doc_id 24177706 directly supports the claim by stating that "TFEB was rapidly activated in murine macrophages upon S. aureus infection and was required for proper transcriptional induction of several proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines." This evidence clearly indicates that TFEB induces transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines in macrophages infected with Staphylococcus aureus.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract by [doc_id 25677651] directly supports the claim by demonstrating that activation of liver X receptors (LXRs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) inhibits apoptotic responses of macrophages to bacterial infection, including Staphylococcus aureus. Additionally, the abstract shows that LXR and RXR agonists induced the expression of antiapoptotic regulators, including AIM/CT2, Bcl-X(L), and Birc1a, which are pro-inflammatory cytokines that contribute to the host defense against infection.
